{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons {{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons {{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons {{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:11 6 4]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons Category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} ]] ]] ]] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:11 6]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:1 revision imported]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}} [[Category:New]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Tsukuyomi-No-Mikoto}}

Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Lunar gods]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Moon Kami in Shinto and Japanese mythology}} {{redirect|Tsukuyomi}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | other_names = Tsukuyomi (ツクヨミ, 月読), Tsukiyomi (ツキヨミ), 月読尊、月弓尊、月夜見尊、月讀尊 | god_of = Kami of the moon | member_of = | image = Shinto-Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto-Old-Artwork.png | caption = Woodblock print of Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto | planet = [[Moon]] | consort = Amaterasu (some myths) | parents = [[Izanagi]] (''[[Kojiki]]'')
[[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]] (''[[Nihon Shoki]]'') | siblings = [[Amaterasu]]
[[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]
(and [[Kamiumi|others]]) | region = Japan | script = 月読命, 月読尊}} {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto'''|ツクヨミノミコト, 月読命}},{{Cite book |author=平藤喜久子 |editor=[[松村一男]]ほか編 |title=神の文化史事典 |publisher=[[白水社]] |date=February 2013 |isbn=978-4-560-08265-2 |page=285 |chapter=スサノオ 建速須佐之男命（記）、素戔嗚尊（紀） }} or simply {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi'''|ツクヨミ, 月読}} or {{Nihongo|'''Tsukiyomi'''|ツキヨミ}},{{Cite web |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/キヨミノミコト(月読尊)-99149 |title=ツキヨミノミコト（月読尊） |work=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典 |publisher=[[コトバンク]] |access-date=2016-09-18}} is the [[List of lunar deities|moon kami]] in [[Japanese mythology]] and the [[Shinto]] religion. The name "Tsukuyomi" is a compound of the [[Old Japanese]] words {{Nihongo||月|tsuku|"moon, month", becoming modern [[Japanese language|Japanese]] ''tsuki''}} and {{Nihongo||読み|yomi|extra="reading, counting"}}.'''1988''', ''国語大辞典（新装版） ''(Kokugo Dai Jiten, Revised Edition) (in Japanese), [[w:Tōkyō|Tōkyō]]: [[w:Shogakukan|Shogakukan]] The ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' mentions this name spelled as {{Nihongo||月弓|Tsukuyumi|extra="moon bow"}}, but this ''yumi'' is likely a variation in pronunciation of ''yomi''. An alternative interpretation is that his name is a combination of {{Nihongo||月夜|tsukiyo|extra="moonlit night"}} and {{nihongo||見|mi|extra="looking, watching"}}. ''-no-Mikoto'' is a common honorific appended to the names of Kami; it may be understood as similar to the English honorific 'the Great'. There is so little known about Tsukuyomi that even their sex is unknown. Still, in ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', Tsukuyomi's name is sometimes rendered as {{Nihongo|'''Tsukuyomi Otoko'''|月讀壮士|extra="moon-reading man"}}, implying that they are male.c. '''759''': ''[[Man'yōshū]]'', volume 7, poem 1372; in [[Old Japanese]]. Text available online [http://etext.lib.virginia.edu/japanese/manyoshu/Man7Yos.html#1372 here]. Tsukuyomi was the second of the {{Nihongo|"three noble children"|三貴子|Mihashira-no-Uzu-no-Miko}} born when [[Izanagi|Izanagi-no-Mikoto]], the kami who created the first land of [[Onogoro Island|Onogoroshima]], was cleansing himself of his kegare while bathing after escaping the [[underworld]] and the clutches of his enraged dead sister, [[Izanami|Izanami-no-Mikoto]]. Tsukuyomi was born when he washed out of Izanagi's right eye.{{cite book | last = Roberts | first = Jeremy | title = Japanese Mythology A To Z | location = New York | publisher = Chelsea House Publishers | year = 2010 | edition = 2nd | isbn = 978-1-60413-435-3 | url = http://www.enryo.ro/carti/Japanese%20mythology%20A%20to%20Z.pdf}} However, in an alternative story, Tsukuyomi was born from a mirror made of white copper in Izanagi's right hand. Tsukuyomi angered [[Amaterasu]] (who in some sources was his wife) when he killed [[Ukemochi]], the megami of food. Amaterasu once sent Tsukuyomi to represent her at a feast presented by Ukemochi. The megami created the food by turning to the ocean and spitting out a fish, then facing a forest and spitting out game, and finally turning to a rice paddy and coughing up a bowl of rice. Tsukuyomi was utterly disgusted by the manner of which the exquisite-looking meal was made in, so he killed her. Amaterasu learned what happened and she was so angry that she refused to ever look at Tsukuyomi again, forever moving to another part of the sky. This is the reason that day and night are never together. This is according to one of the accounts in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Tsukuyomi does not have such significance in the ''[[Kojiki]]'', in which there is a similar tale about [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]] killing a similar food megami named [[Ōgetsuhime]], who is often conflated with Ukemochi. ==Gallery== File:Tsukiyomi-no-miya(Naiku) 02.JPG|The Betsugu Tsukiyomi-no-miya [[Ise Grand Shrine|Sanctuary of Kotaijingu]] (Naiku) at Ise city File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|A [[Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto)|shrine]] to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto at [[Matsunoo Taisha|Matsunoo-taisha]] in [[Kyoto]] ==See also== * [[List of lunar deities]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *{{Commons category-inline}} *Tsukiyomi on the [https://www.historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/tsukiyomi Japanese History Database]. {{Jmyth navbox long}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Personifications]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:11 6 3]] [[Category:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] [[en:Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]]
